perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall communicate any information to the other in violation of the proprietary rights of any third party. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause into the section about third-party proprietary rights obscures its primary function of establishing the legal framework for the entire agreement, making it less prominent and potentially overlooked.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. By burying the governing law clause, the intent of parties regarding which law governs the agreement is obscured, potentially hindering a clear understanding of the agreement and making it more difficult to ascertain the mutual intention of the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw introduces a legal contradiction. According to the Perturbation Explanation, relocating the governing law clause into a section concerning third-party proprietary rights diminishes its prominence, potentially leading it to be overlooked. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code Section 1636 requires contracts to be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties at the time of contracting. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes California Civil Code Section 1636, affirming this requirement. By obscuring the governing law clause, the changed text impedes the clear understanding of the agreement, thereby contradicting the requirement to ascertain the mutual intention of the parties as mandated by California law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that its breach of the Agreement will cause irreparable damage and hereby agrees that the other party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement, as well as such further relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction. Additionally, in the event of a breach by the Recipient, the disclosing party shall be entitled to recover the costs of enforcing this Agreement including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that its breach of the Agreement will cause irreparable damage and hereby agrees that the other party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement, as well as such further relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction. Additionally, in the event of a breach by the Recipient, the disclosing party shall be entitled to recover the costs of enforcing this Agreement including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees. Nothing contained herein shall imply any obligations of either party to proceed with a transaction between the parties, and each party reserves the right to terminate the discussions contemplated hereunder, with or without cause, without any liability for such termination.
perturbation.explanation: The placement of the 'no obligation to proceed' clause directly after the clause detailing remedies for breach suggests that the termination clause somehow limits the remedies available for a breach of confidentiality, when the termination clause should likely have no bearing on this remedy, therefore obscuring the right to attorneys fees.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1717 dictates that in any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees in addition to other costs. By linking the attorneys fees provision with a termination provision, the enforceability is obfuscated.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The added clause regarding termination rights, particularly 'with or without cause, without any liability for such termination', when placed directly after a clause detailing remedies for breach, introduces ambiguity that could weaken the enforceability of the attorney's fees provision, in contradiction with California Civil Code Section 1717. Snippet 1 states that 'the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract... shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs.' By suggesting the contract can be terminated 'without any liability', the added text obfuscates whether the attorney fees provision applies, creating a structural flaw and obscuring enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from the other pursuant to this Agreement or any product utilizing any 3 such data to any country for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will assign or transfer any rights or obligations under this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other party. Neither party shall export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from the other pursuant to this Agreement or any product utilizing any 3 such data to any country for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the export control clause into the section about assignment and transfer of rights buries it within contractual minutiae, obscuring its importance and potentially leading to non-compliance. Its original location was clearer.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.3 outlines the steps for determining export control requirements under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). By placing the export clause in an unexpected location, the likelihood of parties failing to recognize the need to comply with export controls and appropriately classify the technology is increased. This makes it less likely that the parties will follow these steps.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text relocates the export control clause, which, as per the Law Explanation, increases the likelihood of parties failing to recognize and comply with export control requirements. This undermines the regulatory framework established by the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states: 'This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774.' By obscuring the export clause, the changed text makes it less likely that parties will appropriately classify the technology, creating a structural flaw that contradicts the intent of the EAR to ensure compliance with export controls.
